  {
    "content": "You are an expert Linux kernel maintainer. Your goal is to perform a deep, rigorous review of a proposed kernel change to ensure safety, performance, and adherence to subsystem standards.

TOOL USAGE: When you need to gather information using tools, actively batch parallel or independent tool calls into a single response to minimize the number of conversation turns.

<global_review_guidelines>
The following documents contain the official technical patterns, architectural rules, and subsystem-specific guidelines that you MUST adhere to during your review. Use these as the absolute source of truth for identifying anti-patterns and violations.

@technical-patterns.md, @mm-alloc.md

</global_review_guidelines>


Target Commit:
commit 17a0e58a4a5e80c7fe85197550cf2f4a89e7e113
Author: Guilherme G. Piccoli <gpiccoli@igalia.com>
Date:   Wed Mar 18 18:56:33 2026 +0000

    mm/memblock: Add reserve_mem debugfs info
    
    When using the "reserve_mem" parameter, users aim at having an
    area that (hopefully) persists across boots, so pstore infrastructure
    (like ramoops module) can make use of that to save oops/ftrace logs,
    for example.
    
    There is no easy way to determine if this kernel parameter is properly
    set though; the kernel doesn't show information about this memory in
    memblock debugfs, neither in /proc/iomem nor dmesg. This is a relevant
    information for tools like kdumpst[0], to determine if it's reliable
    to use the reserved area as ramoops persistent storage; checking only
    /proc/cmdline is not sufficient as it doesn't tell if the reservation
    effectively succeeded or not.
    
    Add here a new file under memblock debugfs showing properly set memory
    reservations, with name and size as passed to "reserve_mem". Notice that
    if no "reserve_mem=" is passed on command-line or if the reservation
    attempts fail, the file is not created.
    
    [0] https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/kdumpst
    
    Cc: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
    Cc: Mike Rapoport <rppt@kernel.org>
    Cc: Steven Rostedt <rostedt@goodmis.org>
    Cc: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: Guilherme G. Piccoli <gpiccoli@igalia.com>

diff --git a/mm/memblock.c b/mm/memblock.c
index 8dcd40b1c0f0..3bb45e37da92 100644
--- a/mm/memblock.c
+++ b/mm/memblock.c
@@ -17,6 +17,7 @@
 #include <linux/seq_file.h>
 #include <linux/memblock.h>
 #include <linux/mutex.h>
+#include <linux/string_helpers.h>
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_HANDOVER
 #include <linux/libfdt.h>
@@ -2712,7 +2713,8 @@ static int __init reserve_mem(char *p)
 }
 __setup("reserve_mem=", reserve_mem);
 
-#if defined(CONFIG_DEBUG_FS) && defined(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK)
+#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS
+#ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK
 static const char * const flagname[] = {
 	[ilog2(MEMBLOCK_HOTPLUG)] = "HOTPLUG",
 	[ilog2(MEMBLOCK_MIRROR)] = "MIRROR",
@@ -2759,19 +2761,57 @@ static int memblock_debug_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
 }
 DEFINE_SHOW_ATTRIBUTE(memblock_debug);
 
-static int __init memblock_init_debugfs(void)
+static inline void memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(struct dentry *root)
 {
-	struct dentry *root = debugfs_create_dir("memblock", NULL);
-
-	debugfs_create_file("memory", 0444, root,
-			    &memblock.memory, &memblock_debug_fops);
-	debugfs_create_file("reserved", 0444, root,
-			    &memblock.reserved, &memblock_debug_fops);
 #ifdef CONFIG_HAVE_MEMBLOCK_PHYS_MAP
 	debugfs_create_file("physmem", 0444, root, &physmem,
 			    &memblock_debug_fops);
 #endif
+	debugfs_create_file("memory", 0444, root,
+			    &memblock.memory, &memblock_debug_fops);
+	debugfs_create_file("reserved", 0444, root,
+			    &memblock.reserved, &memblock_debug_fops);
+}
+
+#else
+
+static inline void memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(struct dentry *root) { }
+
+#endif /* CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK */
+
+static int memblock_reserve_mem_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
+{
+	struct reserve_mem_table *map;
+	char txtsz[16];
+
+	for (int i = 0; i < reserved_mem_count; i++) {
+		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
+		if (!map->size)
+			continue;
+
+		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
+		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));
+		seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
+	}
+
+	return 0;
+}
+DEFINE_SHOW_ATTRIBUTE(memblock_reserve_mem);
+
+static int __init memblock_init_debugfs(void)
+{
+	struct dentry *root;
+
+	if (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK) && !reserved_mem_count)
+		return 0;
+
+	root = debugfs_create_dir("memblock", NULL);
+
+	if (reserved_mem_count)
+		debugfs_create_file("reserve_mem_param", 0444, root, NULL,
+				    &memblock_reserve_mem_fops);
 
+	memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(root);
 	return 0;
 }
 __initcall(memblock_init_debugfs);

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Race Condition in `memblock_reserve_mem_show`**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `memblock_reserve_mem_show` function, and it seems like there might be a race condition. I see that `reserve_mem_release_by_name` and `reserve_mem_find_by_name` are using the `reserve_mem_lock` mutex, which is good. That means the `reserved_mem_table` can be modified at runtime – specifically, `map->size` can be set to zero when releasing memory.

But here, in `memblock_reserve_mem_show`, I notice it iterates through `reserved_mem_table` without holding the `reserve_mem_lock`. My immediate thought is, "Race condition!"

Let's break it down. If `map->size` is checked in the `if` statement, and then concurrently modified to `0`, calling `string_get_size(0, ...)` is fine. However, what if `reserved_mem_count` changes? Ah, but `reserved_mem_count` is set by `reserved_mem_add`, which is `__init` meaning it only runs during boot. So that's constant after boot. What about `map->name`? Also set at boot and never changed. So it's `map->size` that's the problem.

`map->size` can be changed to 0 by `reserve_mem_release_by_name()`. So if `map->size` is set to 0 concurrently, `string_get_size` will be called with either 0 or the previous value it read. However, reading `map->size` multiple times in `memblock_reserve_mem_show` could be an issue if it changes between reads.

Wait, `if (!map->size)` is the first read. `string_get_size(map->size, ...)` is the second read. So, since `map->size` is read twice without locking, it's possible that `map->size` could be non-zero the first time and 0 the second time. Or, even worse, if it's a partially written value. `map->size` is `phys_addr_t`, which could be 64-bit on a 32-bit system. Writing a 64-bit value to a 32-bit system isn't atomic, and so there's a risk of reading a "torn" value.

But hold on, `reserve_mem_release_by_name` only sets `map->size = 0;`. Okay, so even on a 32-bit system with a 64-bit `phys_addr_t` (e.g., PAE), writing 0 might take two 32-bit stores. Reading it would give either 0 for the high bits and some value for the low bits, or vice-versa. But, since the value is only ever set to 0, a torn read would only result in reading the original size or 0.

So should `reserve_mem_lock` be held? Yes, it's explicitly guarded by `mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock)` in `reserve_mem_find_by_name` and `reserve_mem_release_by_name`. So, to be safe and consistent, I should acquire `reserve_mem_lock` in `memblock_reserve_mem_show`.

Let me just double-check that `reserve_mem_lock` is available. Yes, it's static in `mm/memblock.c`.

Okay, back to the context. I'm looking at `memblock_reserve_mem_show` one more time:

```c
static int memblock_reserve_mem_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;
	char txtsz[16];

	for (int i = 0; i < reserved_mem_count; i++) {
		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
		if (!map->size)
			continue;

		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));

    "thought": "**Analysis of `memblock_reserve_mem_show` Concurrency and Debugfs Behavior**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `memblock_reserve_mem_show` function and its interactions with the `reserved_mem_table` data structure. First, I need to make sure the buffer `txtsz` is large enough to hold the output from `string_get_size`, which seems to be okay given the context in several other kernel files where similar-sized buffers are used, and the documentation for `string_get_size`. Now, the concurrency aspect is what concerns me most.

My primary thought is whether `reserved_mem_count` is being handled atomically or with some kind of locking. It looks like `reserved_mem_count` is only incremented during initialization. It's essentially static after that point. However, `map->size` within the `reserved_mem_table` *can* be modified concurrently by `reserve_mem_release_by_name`. Now, if we are on a 32-bit architecture, a torn read of `map->size` could result in garbage, which is an issue when you want reliable data in debugfs. While this is just a text output, I think we should definitely address this. Using `READ_ONCE` or, even better, the `reserve_mem_lock` to protect access to `map->size` looks like the right approach, since the lock is specifically intended for protecting the `reserved_mem_table`.

I also need to make sure the debugfs initialization is correct. If `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is disabled, the `memblock` directory will only be created if `reserved_mem_count` is greater than zero. The code behaves as intended. And even if `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is disabled, `memblock` structures are freed but `reserved_mem_table` and `reserved_mem_count` are not marked `__initdata`, and functions not marked `__init` remain in the kernel, so the debugfs should still function correctly.

Now, going back to concurrency... the double read is problematic. We're reading `map->size` twice, once to check if it's zero and then to pass it to `string_get_size`. If `map->size` is zeroed between the reads, we could end up calling `string_get_size` with zero. This isn't a *bug*, per se, but it does mean we might output a '0 B' size for a memory region that was just released, which is inconsistent. To avoid this, and to prevent potential torn reads on 32-bit architectures, we *need* to acquire `reserve_mem_lock` before reading `map->size`. The mutex is already defined in `mm/memblock.c`! Also, `seq_printf` doesn't prevent holding the mutex.

I've got a formal report: "Missing lock in `memblock_reserve_mem_show`: The function iterates over `reserved_mem_table` and reads `map->size` without holding `reserve_mem_lock`, which is used in `reserve_mem_release_by_name` and `reserve_mem_find_by_name`. This can lead to race conditions where `map->size` is zeroed concurrently, leading to tore reads on 32-bit architectures (since `phys_addr_t` is 64-bit) or inconsistent debugfs output (e.g. `size` evaluates to true in `if (!map->size)`, but is 0 when passed to `string_get_size`)."

Finally, I remember that memblock tests in `tools/testing/memblock` will fail because `string_get_size` isn't implemented in the stub! I need to trace this in detail. If `memblock_reserve_mem_show` calls `string_get_size()` in the tests, then the test build will fail because the function isn't implemented. So, the tests will have to be modified.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Build Failure in the Memblock Simulator**

Okay, first thought: `string_get_size` isn't declared in `tools/testing/memblock/linux/string_helpers.h`. Let's re-examine the patch. Yep, it's just a header stub. Now, `mm/memblock.c` *does* call `string_get_size`, and it includes `<linux/string_helpers.h>`.  In the kernel build, that resolves to the standard kernel header. However, in the memblock simulator, that include will resolve to `tools/testing/memblock/linux/string_helpers.h`.

If `string_get_size` is not declared in the simulator's header, then the compilation of `memblock.c` should throw an implicit declaration error or warning, which is likely an error given `-Wall` in the `CFLAGS` (specifically the `-Wimplicit-function-declaration` flag which is enabled by `-Wall`). Since it is C99, implicit function declarations should be errors. This looks like a definite build break.

And, looking at the patch, the header stub indeed has *no* prototype for `string_get_size`.

Furthermore, it uses `STRING_UNITS_2`. That's an enumerator defined in *kernel's* `include/linux/string_helpers.h`.  If we just stub the header, and don't define `STRING_UNITS_2`, `memblock.c` is going to error with "error: 'STRING_UNITS_2' undeclared".

Let's quickly check the kernel's `include/linux/string_helpers.h` to confirm the enum: Yep, `STRING_UNITS_2` is defined in the kernel header, and it won't be in the stub. So, the simulator build *will* fail if it doesn't define `string_get_size()` and `STRING_UNITS_2`.

Now, the code that calls `string_get_size` is in `memblock_reserve_mem_show()`, which is guarded by `#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS`. I need to check the `tools/testing/memblock/Makefile` or `common.h` to see if `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` is defined in the memblock simulator build. If it is *not* defined, then the failure is more subtle, as the `memblock_reserve_mem_show` will not be compiled!

The `map->size` field can be modified concurrently at runtime: `reserve_mem_release_by_name()` explicitly acquires `reserve_mem_lock` to set `map->size = 0` when releasing reservations. Because `memblock_reserve_mem_show()` does not take this lock, a race condition exists. On 32-bit systems with 64-bit physical addresses (`CONFIG_PHYS_ADDR_T_64BIT`), writing and reading a 64-bit value is not atomic. If `map->size` is zeroed concurrently, a torn read can occur, passing a corrupted size to `string_get_size()` and displaying garbage in debugfs. Furthermore, even on 64-bit systems, the first read might evaluate to true while the second read evaluates to 0, resulting in an inconsistent '0 B' output for an already-released region. The iteration block should be protected using `guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock);` to ensure consistent and tear-free reads."

Okay, so I've been carefully reviewing this patch, and here's my assessment. First off, it appears the code correctly handles the conditional compilation depending on `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS`. Since the memblock simulator doesn't define `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS`, `memblock_reserve_mem_show` *isn't* compiled during tests, which means that the test doesn't even use `string_get_size`, so the stub is sufficient to allow a successful build, and the commit message is accurate. The code structure for debugfs seems to be correctly refactored, and it now conditionally creates the debugfs directory and file depending on configuration options and if reservations exist. If `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is disabled, the code only creates `reserve_mem_param` rather than the standard memblock debugfs files, matching old behavior. And the logic to only show reservations that have *succeeded* via `reserve_mem=` seems sound, matching the commit message about `/proc/cmdline` not indicating success, and it is handled via checks inside `memblock_reserve_mem_show` with `if (!map->size)`. The functions and the `reserve_mem_table` is protected using mutex. ```c
static int memblock_reserve_mem_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;
	char txtsz[16];

	for (int i = 0; i < reserved_mem_count; i++) {
		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
		if (!map->size)
			continue;

		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));
		seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
	}

	return 0;
}
```

My thought is, is `map->size` safely accessed here? It's a `phys_addr_t`, which could be 32 or 64-bit. `string_get_size` takes a `u64 size` argument, so the code should work but I need to make sure access is safe.

I notice that `reserve_mem_release_by_name()` modifies `map->size` concurrently:

```c
int reserve_mem_release_by_name(const char *name)
{
...
	guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock);
	map = reserve_mem_find_by_name_nolock(name);
...
	map->size = 0;
}
```

This is where the potential for a race is. If a concurrent thread is calling `reserve_mem_release_by_name()` and sets `map->size` to 0, and we're reading `map->size` in `memblock_reserve_mem_show()`, we could encounter a torn read on 32-bit systems where `phys_addr_t` is 64-bit (like ARM with LPAE or x86 with PAE). That means a read might return a partially updated value, perhaps garbage.

If a torn read of 0 occurs, that would mean either the high 32 bits or low 32 bits are read as 0 while the other half still has the old value. If the original size was `0x00000000_10000000`, the torn read would either be the original size or 0. If it was 4GB (`0x00000001_00000000`), a torn read could yield either the old value or 0, depending on write order.

However, even though this could lead to incorrect output for a brief period, it won't crash the system. Still, this is technically a data race – Undefined Behavior in C. I see that `reserve_mem_lock` exists to protect `reserve_mem_table`, but it's not being used in `memblock_reserve_mem_show()`. That's strange. Given that the lock *is* in place, I'd strongly suggest using the mutex or using `READ_ONCE` and `WRITE_ONCE` for memory accesses for safety. This is a clear oversight.

Okay, so I'm looking at this `memblock_reserve_mem_show()` function and it's using `string_get_size` to format the size of reserved memory regions. I see that `string_get_size` is consistently called with a buffer size of, say, 16 bytes, which seems reasonable given the typical sizes involved. Specifically, I'm concerned about the lack of any obvious locking mechanism around the access to the `reserved_mem_table`. Another process could, in theory, call `reserve_mem_release_by_name()` and modify that `map->size` value *between* my read and the `string_get_size` call. If that happens right after my `if (!map->size)` check, I could end up calling `string_get_size(0, ...)` which is harmless from a crash perspective.

However, consider that `map->size` is a 64-bit value, especially on a 32-bit architecture. This means a *torn read* is a possibility. The lower 32 bits might be read as zero, while the upper bits contain an old value. This would lead to a completely incorrect size being passed to `string_get_size`. It might be visually wrong but functionally safe.

The simplest solution here is to guard access to `reserved_mem_table` with `reserve_mem_lock`. I need to ensure that the code is protected. I'll need to add `guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock);` inside `memblock_reserve_mem_show()`.

    "thought": "**My Analysis of the `reserve_mem` Debugfs Patch**

Okay, let's break this down. First, I checked how `debugfs_create_file` handles errors, specifically the case where the parent directory creation fails.  The documentation is clear: if `debugfs_create_dir` fails, it's fine, the file creation will return an error, but it won't try to create it in the root directory.  So, no immediate concerns there.

Now, focusing on the core logic, I zeroed in on the potential data race. My primary concern is that `memblock_reserve_mem_show`, the function displaying the reserved memory information via debugfs, iterates through the `reserved_mem_table` and reads `map->size` without taking the `reserve_mem_lock`. This is where the issue lies.

My reasoning is straightforward: `reserve_mem_release_by_name()` *can* be called concurrently – perhaps from a tracepoint or similar – to release a reserved region.  This function *does* hold `reserve_mem_lock` before setting `map->size = 0`. Since `memblock_reserve_mem_show` iterates over the same table and reads `map->size` multiple times (first, checking `!map->size`, then passing it to `string_get_size()`) without acquiring the `reserve_mem_lock` *or* using `READ_ONCE`, we have a classic data race. Ah! In the original code, the entire `debugfs` stuff was inside `#if defined(CONFIG_DEBUG_FS) && defined(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK)`.
Now, `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` enables `memblock_init_debugfs`.
But wait! If `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is NOT defined, is `memblock_debug_show` still defined?
No! Because `memblock_debug_show` is inside `#ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK`.
Let's look at the patch:
```c
-#if defined(CONFIG_DEBUG_FS) && defined(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK)
+#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS
+#ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK
 static const char * const flagname[] = {
 	[ilog2(MEMBLOCK_HOTPLUG)] = "HOTPLUG",
 	[ilog2(MEMBLOCK_MIRROR)] = "MIRROR",
...
@@ -2759,19 +2761,57 @@ static int memblock_debug_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
 }
 DEFINE_SHOW_ATTRIBUTE(memblock_debug);
 
-static int __init memblock_init_debugfs(void)
+static inline void memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(struct dentry *root)
 {
...
+#else
+
+static inline void memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(struct dentry *root) { }
+
+#endif /* CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK */
```
This is perfect. If `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is disabled, we don't compile `memblock_debug_show` or `memblock_debugfs_make_dirs`, but we DO compile `memblock_reserve_mem_show`!

Wait, is there any chance that `reserved_mem_table` is freed?
No, it's a global statically allocated array.

Let's check the iteration in `memblock_reserve_mem_show` again:
```c
	for (int i = 0; i < reserved_mem_count; i++) {
		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
		if (!map->size)
			continue;

		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));
		seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
	}
```
If `reserve_mem_release_by_name` (or whatever sets `size = 0`) runs concurrently, it could set `size` to 0 after `if (!map->size)`.
Then `string_get_size` is called with `0`. It formats "0 B" (or similar). That's perfectly safe.
What if `map->name` is changed?
`map->name` is never changed after `reserve_mem_add`.
The only field that can change is `size` in `reserve_mem_release_by_name` (which sets it to 0).
Actually, `free_reserved_area` frees the physical memory, and then `map->size` becomes 0.
So reading `map->name` and `map->size` concurrently is benign.

Wait, what about `seq_printf` being used inside the loop?
```c
seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
```
Is `map->name` guaranteed to be null-terminated?
```c
static void __init reserved_mem_add(phys_addr_t start, phys_addr_t size,
				   const char *name)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;

	map = &reserved_mem_table[reserved_mem_count++];
	map->start = start;
	map->size = size;
	strscpy(map->name, name);
}
```
Yes, `strscpy` guarantees null-termination. (Wait, `strscpy(dest, src)` uses the size of `dest` array automatically if it's an array, because it's a macro or inline function that gets `sizeof()` in some kernels? Wait, in Linux kernel, `strscpy` takes 3 arguments or 2? Let's check:

    "content": "推理
Ah, so `strscpy` requires `sizeof(dest)` usually as the 3rd argument, unless it's a wrapper macro that calculates it. Wait, `reserve_mem` uses `strscpy(map->name, name);` without the third argument?
Let's see:
```c
static void __init reserved_mem_add(phys_addr_t start, phys_addr_t size,
				   const char *name)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;

	map = &reserved_mem_table[reserved_mem_count++];
	map->start = start;
	map->size = size;
	strscpy(map->name, name);
}
```
Wait, in `include/linux/string.h`, `strscpy` is defined as a macro in recent kernels:
```c
#define strscpy(dest, src, ...) \
	__strscpy(dest, src, ##__VA_ARGS__)
```
Wait, actually, in 6.8+ or so, `strscpy` can be called with 2 arguments, and it infers the size using `__builtin_object_size`.
Yes, this is an established kernel API update!

So `map->name` is perfectly null-terminated.

Wait, is there any problem if `string_get_size` is called with an invalid `size`?
`string_get_size` handles any `u64` size and formats it properly.

What about `reserved_mem_count` being read without lock?
`reserved_mem_count` is statically allocated, initialized to 0, incremented at `__init` during `reserve_mem_add`.
It is NEVER decremented.
So reading `reserved_mem_count` at runtime without a lock is perfectly safe because it is not changed after init.

What about `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` vs `debugfs` directory creation?
Before this patch, `memblock` debugfs directory was only created if `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` was set.
If a system didn't have `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK`, no `memblock` dir existed.
With this patch, the `memblock` dir is created if `reserved_mem_count > 0` even if `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is not set.
This makes sense, because `reserve_mem_param` feature relies on the dir.
And if `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is set, `memblock_debugfs_make_dirs` creates the other files. If it's not set, it's a no-op.

Is there any leak with `seq_file`?
`DEFINE_SHOW_ATTRIBUTE(memblock_reserve_mem)` expands to standard seq_file operations with open/read/llseek/release, which do not leak memory.
`memblock_reserve_mem_show` iterates over elements.

Wait, what if `IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK)` is false, and `reserved_mem_count` is 0?
```c
	if (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK) && !reserved_mem_count)
		return 0;
```
It returns 0. The `memblock` dir is not created. This maintains the old behavior when `!CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK`, and avoids creating an empty dir.
What if `IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK)` is true, and `reserved_mem_count` is 0?
The `if` condition is false.
It proceeds to create `memblock` dir.
It skips `debugfs_create_file("reserve_mem_param", ...)` because `reserved_mem_count` is 0.
Then it calls `memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(root)` which creates "memory", "reserved", etc.
This matches the exact original behavior.

Wait, the original `memblock_init_debugfs` also had `__initcall`.
But wait! If `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is false, `memblock` is discarded after boot?
Let's check `memblock_discard`.
```c
#ifndef CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK
/**
 * memblock_discard - discard memory and reserved arrays if they were allocated
 */
void __init memblock_discard(void)
{ ... }
```
Wait! Does `memblock_discard` discard `reserved_mem_table`?
I searched for `reserved_mem_table` and there's no mention of it being discarded.
It's just an array:
```c
static struct reserve_mem_table reserved_mem_table[RESERVE_MEM_MAX_ENTRIES];
```
It does not have `__initdata`, so it is kept in memory.

Wait, in `memblock_reserve_mem_show`:
```c
static int memblock_reserve_mem_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;
	char txtsz[16];

	for (int i = 0; i < reserved_mem_count; i++) {
		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
		if (!map->size)
			continue;

		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));
		seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
	}

	return 0;
}
```
Is there ANY issue with this logic?
- `txtsz` is `char txtsz[16]`.
- `string_get_size` will format the size into `txtsz`.
Is 16 enough?
Let's see what `string_get_size` outputs.
Format: `1023.99 EiB` is 11 chars + null terminator = 12 bytes.
So 16 is enough.

Wait, I need to check if `string_get_size` is safe.
`string_get_size(u64 size, u64 blk_size, const enum string_size_units units, char *buf, int len);`
Wait, what if `map->size` is not a multiple of block size? It's fine.
What if `size` is large?
If `txtsz` is too small, `string_get_size` uses `snprintf`, which prevents buffer overflow, but may truncate. `txtsz` is large enough.

Let's check `string_get_size` signature.
```c
void string_get_size(u64 size, u64 blk_size, const enum string_size_units units, char *buf, int len);
```
So `sizeof(txtsz)` is passed as `len`. This is correct.

Wait... Look at the `tools/testing/memblock/linux/string_helpers.h` file added in the patch.

Ah wait, `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` IS defined or NOT defined.
Wait, if it's NOT defined, `debugfs_create_dir` is defined as:

	return ERR_PTR(-ENODEV);

```
And `string_get_size` is from `include/linux/string_helpers.h`.
BUT wait, in `tools/testing/memblock/linux/string_helpers.h` it was explicitly stubbed because the tools testing framework for memblock does not compile with the real `string_helpers.h`.
Let's look at `tools/testing/memblock/linux/string_helpers.h`.
```c
/* SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0 */
#ifndef _LINUX_STRING_HELPERS_H_
#define _LINUX_STRING_HELPERS_H_

/*
 * Header stub to avoid test build breakage; we don't need to
 * actually implement string_get_size() as it's not used in the tests.
 */

#endif
```
If it's not implemented, but it IS USED in `memblock.c` (inside `memblock_reserve_mem_show`), then won't there be a linking error in the test suite?
Wait! Is `memblock_reserve_mem_show` compiled in the test suite?
Let's see if `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` is defined in `tools/testing/memblock`.
In `tools/testing/memblock/linux/kconfig.h`:
Does it define `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS`?
If `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` is NOT defined in tests, then `#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` block is NOT compiled, so `memblock_reserve_mem_show` is NOT compiled, and thus `string_get_size` is NOT referenced in the object file!
Then the stub is perfectly fine because the compiler won't even see a call to `string_get_size` and the linker won't complain.
But wait! What if `string_get_size` is referenced inside `#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS`? The test compilation environment would fail at compile time due to implicit declaration of `string_get_size`!
Because if `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` IS defined in the test environment, the compiler will complain. If it is NOT defined, then the `#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` block is entirely skipped.
Wait, if `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` is NOT defined in the test suite, then why did the author add `tools/testing/memblock/linux/string_helpers.h`?
Because `mm/memblock.c` has `#include <linux/string_helpers.h>` AT THE TOP!
```c

+#include <linux/string_helpers.h>
```
So when `mm/memblock.c` is compiled in `tools/testing/memblock`, it includes `<linux/string_helpers.h>`. Since that file doesn't exist in the test environment's include path, the compile would fail with "file not found".
So the author added a dummy `string_helpers.h` in `tools/testing/memblock/linux/`.
But wait! If the test suite DOES compile `memblock_reserve_mem_show` (maybe `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` IS defined in tests?!), then the test suite would fail with "implicit declaration of function string_get_size".

    "content": "推理
Since `CONFIG_DEBUG_FS` is NOT defined in `tools/testing/memblock/`, the entire block:
```c
#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS
...
static int memblock_reserve_mem_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
...
static int __init memblock_init_debugfs(void)
...
#endif
```
will be excluded from the test suite build.
Thus, `string_get_size` will NOT be called in the test suite.
The header stub `tools/testing/memblock/linux/string_helpers.h` is just there so that `#include <linux/string_helpers.h>` at the top of `memblock.c` doesn't fail with "No such file or directory".
This is perfectly correct and well-thought-out by the author.

Wait, are there any UAF or race conditions?
As established, `reserved_mem_count` is only updated in `__init`, meaning after boot it is a read-only variable.
`reserved_mem_table` has entries appended in `__init`. During runtime, elements are modified ONLY by `reserve_mem_release_by_name`, which sets `map->size = 0` (inside a lock, but we read it outside).
`memblock_reserve_mem_show` reads `map->size` without the lock:
```c
		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
		if (!map->size)
			continue;

		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));
		seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
```
If `reserve_mem_release_by_name` modifies `map->size` concurrently to 0:
1. `!map->size` is false (was not zero).
2. It calls `string_get_size` with `size = 0`.
3. `string_get_size` calculates "0 B".
4. `seq_printf` prints `name` and "0 B".
`name` is never mutated after `__init`.
This is completely safe in practice because `map->size` is a `phys_addr_t` (either 32-bit or 64-bit), and reading it racily might give an old value or 0, but it won't crash the kernel. Data races are typically flagged by KCSAN, so using `READ_ONCE(map->size)` would be cleaner, but it's debugfs, so maintainers generally accept this minor slack or rely on KCSAN not being enabled in production. Should I flag the missing lock / `READ_ONCE`?
Maybe. If a data race can cause a corrupted size read (e.g. tearing on a 32-bit system reading a 64-bit `phys_addr_t`), `string_get_size` might be called with a garbage value. But `string_get_size` handles any `u64` size perfectly (it just formats it as Exabytes, Petabytes, etc.). So it's not a security issue, just potentially a weird debugfs output. But wait, `reserve_mem_release_by_name` writes 0 to `map->size`. The write of 0 might tear.
Is it worth flagging? The prompt says "Do NOT dismiss concerns just because you assume the surrounding system or caller handles it perfectly. Do not be overly charitable to the existing code."
But wait, `reserve_mem_lock` is held by the writer:
```c
	guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock);
	map = reserve_mem_find_by_name_nolock(name);
...
	map->size = 0;
```
If the reader doesn't hold `reserve_mem_lock`, KCSAN WILL report a data race. Since we have a lock dedicated to `reserve_mem` (`reserve_mem_lock`), we should definitely use it!
Wait, look at `reserve_mem_find_by_name`:
```c
int reserve_mem_find_by_name(const char *name, phys_addr_t *start, phys_addr_t *size)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;

	guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock);
	map = reserve_mem_find_by_name_nolock(name);
	if (!map)
		return 0;

	*start = map->start;
	*size = map->size;
	return 1;
}
```
It uses `guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock)`.
Why doesn't `memblock_reserve_mem_show` use it? It just iterates over the table without locking!
```c
static int memblock_reserve_mem_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;
	char txtsz[16];

    // MISSING LOCK HERE
	for (int i = 0; i < reserved_mem_count; i++) {
		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
		if (!map->size)
			continue;

		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));
		seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
	}

	return 0;
}
```
Is this an omission? Yes, it is!
If `reserve_mem_lock` is meant to protect concurrent accesses to `reserved_mem_table`, reading it in `memblock_reserve_mem_show` without holding `reserve_mem_lock` is a textbook concurrency bug. The fact that the list only shrinks (sets `size` to 0) doesn't excuse tearing. `phys_addr_t` is 64-bit on 32-bit systems with PAE. A torn read of `map->size` from `0x0000000100000000` to `0` could result in `0x0000000100000000` or `0x0000000000000000` or `0x0000000100000000`. Wait, that's not bad. What if it was `0x00000000FFFFFFFF` and it becomes `0`? A torn read could yield `0x00000000FFFFFFFF` or `0` or `0x0000000000000000` or `0xFFFFFFFF00000000`. The last one is very large! It's a garbage value.
So this is a valid data race concern. The lock should be acquired.
`guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock);` can simply be added at the beginning of `memblock_reserve_mem_show`.

Let's carefully verify where `reserve_mem_lock` is defined and if it is accessible from `memblock_reserve_mem_show`.
Both are in `mm/memblock.c`.
```c
static DEFINE_MUTEX(reserve_mem_lock);
```
So yes, it is directly accessible. The lock is not held in `memblock_reserve_mem_show`, causing a data race with `reserve_mem_release_by_name`.

Is there any other issue?
Let's check `memblock_init_debugfs`.
```c
static int __init memblock_init_debugfs(void)
{
	struct dentry *root;

	if (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK) && !reserved_mem_count)
		return 0;

	root = debugfs_create_dir("memblock", NULL);

	if (reserved_mem_count)
		debugfs_create_file("reserve_mem_param", 0444, root, NULL,
				    &memblock_reserve_mem_fops);

	memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(root);
	return 0;
}
```
Wait! What if `debugfs_create_dir` fails and returns an error pointer?
Then `root` is an error pointer (e.g., `-ENODEV`).
`debugfs_create_file` takes `root`. If `root` is an error pointer, `debugfs_create_file` gracefully handles it, usually falling back to the debugfs root or failing immediately.
Actually, in debugfs, we are encouraged NOT to check the return value of `debugfs_create_dir` and `debugfs_create_file`.
See `Documentation/filesystems/debugfs.rst`: "In general, there is no need to check the return value of debugfs_create_*".
So that's fine.

What about `memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(root)`?
```c
static inline void memblock_debugfs_make_dirs(struct dentry *root)
{
#ifdef CONFIG_HAVE_MEMBLOCK_PHYS_MAP
	debugfs_create_file("physmem", 0444, root, &physmem,
			    &memblock_debug_fops);
#endif
	debugfs_create_file("memory", 0444, root,
			    &memblock.memory, &memblock_debug_fops);
	debugfs_create_file("reserved", 0444, root,
			    &memblock.reserved, &memblock_debug_fops);
}
```
This is also fine.

Wait, is there any problem with `reserved_mem_count` inside `memblock_init_debugfs`?
```c
	if (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK) && !reserved_mem_count)
		return 0;
```
It is safe.

Let's double check `string_get_size` parameters.
`string_get_size(u64 size, u64 blk_size, const enum string_size_units units, char *buf, int len)`
The patch passes:
`string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));`
`map->size` is `phys_addr_t`, which gets implicitly promoted/cast to `u64`.
`1` is `blk_size`.
`STRING_UNITS_2` means powers of 1024 (KiB, MiB, etc.).
`txtsz` is the buffer.
`sizeof(txtsz)` is 16.
This is absolutely correct.

Wait... Look at the `reserve_mem_table` loop in `memblock_reserve_mem_show` again.
```c
static int memblock_reserve_mem_show(struct seq_file *m, void *private)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;
	char txtsz[16];

	for (int i = 0; i < reserved_mem_count; i++) {
		map = &reserved_mem_table[i];
		if (!map->size)
			continue;

		memset(txtsz, 0, sizeof(txtsz));
		string_get_size(map->size, 1, STRING_UNITS_2, txtsz, sizeof(txtsz));
		seq_printf(m, "%s\t\t(%s)\n", map->name, txtsz);
	}

	return 0;
}
```
Is there a memory leak? No.
Is there an uninitialized variable? `txtsz` is memset to 0, which is good (although not strictly required).
Is there a use-after-free? `reserved_mem_table` is a static global array.

Wait, what if `reserve_mem_count` changes?
We established it only increases in `__init`, never decreases. So it's safe to read.

Are there any other callers that modify `map->size`?
`reserve_mem_release_by_name` sets `map->size = 0`.
Are there any callers that modify `map->name`?
No, `reserve_mem_release_by_name` does not clear `map->name`.

So the ONLY concurrency issue is `map->size` being read while being modified to `0` by `reserve_mem_release_by_name` on a 32-bit architecture with 64-bit `phys_addr_t`, which can cause a torn read. Since `reserve_mem_lock` exists specifically for this struct, not using it is a defect.

Let's look at `reserve_mem_release_by_name` again:
```c
int reserve_mem_release_by_name(const char *name)
{
	char buf[RESERVE_MEM_NAME_SIZE + 12];
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;
	void *start, *end;

	guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock);
	map = reserve_mem_find_by_name_nolock(name);
	if (!map)
		return 0;

	start = phys_to_virt(map->start);
	end = start + map->size - 1;
	snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "reserve_mem:%s", name);
	free_reserved_area(start, end, 0, buf);
	map->size = 0;

	return 1;
}
```
It holds `reserve_mem_lock` for the whole duration.
If `memblock_reserve_mem_show` runs simultaneously:
It might read `map->size` while `free_reserved_area` is running, before `map->size = 0`.
This means it displays a size that is being freed. This is standard TOCTOU, but acceptable for debugfs.
However, a torn read of `map->size` (e.g. `map->size = 0` being written while `map->size` is read) can be solved by acquiring `reserve_mem_lock`.

Wait, what about `reserve_mem` itself?
```c
static int __init reserve_mem(char *p)
{
...
	if (reserve_mem_find_by_name(name, &start, &tmp)) {
...
```
`reserve_mem_find_by_name` grabs the mutex, but `reserve_mem` is `__init` so it runs single-threaded mostly, but still it's safe.

What else?
Let's review the `#ifdef` logic once more.
```c
-#if defined(CONFIG_DEBUG_FS) && defined(CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK)
+#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS
+#ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK
```
`__initcall(memblock_init_debugfs);` is moved outside of `#ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK`.
Is there any chance `memblock_init_debugfs` accesses something freed if `CONFIG_ARCH_KEEP_MEMBLOCK` is false?
```c
static int __init memblock_init_debugfs(void)

Concern 1:
Type: Data Race / Concurrency
Description: `memblock_reserve_mem_show` accesses `reserved_mem_table` and its elements (specifically `map->size`) without holding `reserve_mem_lock`.
Reasoning: Concurrent calls to `reserve_mem_release_by_name` acquire `reserve_mem_lock` to modify the region and clear `map->size` to `0`. On 32-bit systems where `phys_addr_t` is 64-bit (PAE), a torn read of `map->size` can occur if read concurrently with a 0-write, leading to garbage size values being passed to `string_get_size` and displayed. Even on 64-bit systems, this constitutes a data race. Adding `guard(mutex)(&reserve_mem_lock)` at the beginning of `memblock_reserve_mem_show` or within the loop resolves this concurrency issue.

Okay, let's see. I was looking at `reserved_mem_add`. My first thought was, "Is there a bounds check in there for `reserved_mem_count` against `RESERVE_MEM_MAX_ENTRIES`?" Looking at the code:

```c
static void __init reserved_mem_add(phys_addr_t start, phys_addr_t size,
				   const char *name)
{
	struct reserve_mem_table *map;

	map = &reserved_mem_table[reserved_mem_count++];
	map->start = start;
	map->size = size;
	strscpy(map->name, name);

Thus, a TOCTOU race condition exists, where a concurrent call to `reserve_mem_release_by_name()` can zero out the value of `map->size` between the check and the call to `string_get_size()`. Furthermore, on 32-bit arches with 64 bit pointers this can yield a torn read.

I think the fix here is to guard the entire loop within `memblock_reserve_mem_show()` with `reserve_mem_lock`. This should address the race condition.  I've framed it as a question to maintain the usual LKML tone. This should be a straightforward fix.
